title: Keeping a culture breathing
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: Oxygen is the sneaky bottleneck of every aerobic fermentation: it dissolves poorly (only ~8 mg/L saturates water), yet a dense culture can consume it in seconds. Start air-saturated and switch the cells on, and the dissolved O₂ plunges within a few seconds to a new balance where supply meets demand — here 4 mg/L, half saturation, on a sub-minute time constant of 1/kLa. Too weak a kLa and it would crash to zero and starve the cells. This gas–liquid transfer is why bioreactors are stirred so hard and why scale-up is really an oxygen-transfer problem.
